content.content:
1	# Quest-helper CLI wrapper for nsg
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Build a small CLI that wraps `nsg` with a quest-shaped interface for Coco (and other LLM agents working on quests). The wrapper encodes the file-canonical quest conventions as commands, so individual rituals (create, open for edit, close, migrate) can't drift or be executed out of order. This is a sanctum-side convenience layer, not a framework feature — it exists because the file-canonical quest model (parent quest w-mnt0jin1) is a convention layered on top of `nsg`, and discipline alone may not be enough to keep the convention consistent over time.
6	
7	## Status
8	
9	deferred — captured as the future escape hatch while we ship the file-canonical quest convention without any CLI scaffolding. Revisit only if the convention proves too easy to get wrong in practice.
10	
11	## Next Steps
12	
13	Wait for the parent quest (w-mnt0jin1) to ship and land. Then watch for drift: Coco forgetting to snapshot on closure, row bodies getting overwritten by accident, migration scripts needing re-runs, files appearing in the wrong directory, stale files left behind after closure. If any of those become recurring pain, open this quest and scope the wrapper.
14	
15	When the time comes, the likely command shape is:
16	
17	- `quest new --title ... --goal ... [--parent <id>]` — creates the writ row with just the Goal + pointer warning, writes the initial file at `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<id>.md` with the full template, prints the file path.
18	- `quest path <id>` — prints the canonical file path for a quest. Useful for scripts and editor integration.
19	- `quest close <id> [--complete|--cancel|--fail]` — reads the file, snapshots it into the row body via `nsg writ edit`, transitions status via `nsg writ complete|cancel|fail`, unlinks the file. Atomic-ish from the caller's view.
20	- `quest migrate` — idempotent one-shot that reconciles row bodies and files for any drift. Safe to re-run.
21	- `quest list` — thin wrapper around `nsg writ list --type quest` with friendlier output (title + status + one-line Goal preview).
22	
23	Implementation language: probably a bash script or a small Node script that shells out to `nsg`. No framework changes. Lives in the sanctum (`/workspace/nexus-mk2/bin/quest.sh` or similar).
24	
25	## Context
26	
27	Parent quest w-mnt0jin1 (File-canonical quest bodies) resolves by shipping a pure-convention layer: the writ row holds just the Goal plus a pointer warning comment; the living body lives in a real file at `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<id>.md`; git in the vibers guild provides history and conflict semantics; closure is a manual Coco ritual (snapshot file into row → complete writ → rm file).
28	
29	That model deliberately has no framework support — no new `nsg` subcommands, no schema changes, no CLI hydration. It is \"us-specific, not framework-proven\" (Sean's framing, 2026-04-10). The tradeoff is that Coco has to remember the ritual every time, and if a future session (or script, or other agent) calls `nsg writ edit --body` on a live quest without reading the visible warning in the row body, data can drift: the file and the row get out of sync, and on closure the wrong version wins.
30	
31	This wrapper is the escape hatch for that risk. We're deferring it until the pain shows up — the alternative (building it now) violates the us-specific framing and locks in a CLI shape before we've learned what ergonomics actually matter.
32	
33	Watch items during the parent quest's initial life:
34	
35	- Does Coco reliably follow the close ritual end-to-end?
36	- Does the row-body warning comment actually prevent accidental edits?
37	- Do migration re-runs surface drift, or is drift rare enough to not matter?
38	- Does any other agent (Astrolabe, ethnographer, future animas) try to touch quest writs in a way that conflicts with the convention?
39	
40	If the answers are \"yes, yes, no, no,\" the wrapper stays deferred forever. If any of them flip, open this quest and build the thing.
41	
42	## References

# Quest-helper CLI wrapper for nsg

## Goal

Build a small CLI that wraps `nsg` with a quest-shaped interface for Coco (and other LLM agents working on quests). The wrapper encodes the file-canonical quest conventions as commands, so individual rituals (create, open for edit, close, migrate) can't drift or be executed out of order. This is a sanctum-side convenience layer, not a framework feature — it exists because the file-canonical quest model (parent quest w-mnt0jin1) is a convention layered on top of `nsg`, and discipline alone may not be enough to keep the convention consistent over time.

## Status

deferred — captured as the future escape hatch while we ship the file-canonical quest convention without any CLI scaffolding. Revisit only if the convention proves too easy to get wrong in practice.

## Next Steps

Wait for the parent quest (w-mnt0jin1) to ship and land. Then watch for drift: Coco forgetting to snapshot on closure, row bodies getting overwritten by accident, migration scripts needing re-runs, files appearing in the wrong directory, stale files left behind after closure. If any of those become recurring pain, open this quest and scope the wrapper.

When the time comes, the likely command shape is:

- `quest new --title ... --goal ... [--parent <id>]` — creates the writ row with just the Goal + pointer warning, writes the initial file at `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<id>.md` with the full template, prints the file path.
- `quest path <id>` — prints the canonical file path for a quest. Useful for scripts and editor integration.
- `quest close <id> [--complete|--cancel|--fail]` — reads the file, snapshots it into the row body via `nsg writ edit`, transitions status via `nsg writ complete|cancel|fail`, unlinks the file. Atomic-ish from the caller's view.
- `quest migrate` — idempotent one-shot that reconciles row bodies and files for any drift. Safe to re-run.
- `quest list` — thin wrapper around `nsg writ list --type quest` with friendlier output (title + status + one-line Goal preview).

Implementation language: probably a bash script or a small Node script that shells out to `nsg`. No framework changes. Lives in the sanctum (`/workspace/nexus-mk2/bin/quest.sh` or similar).

## Context

Parent quest w-mnt0jin1 (File-canonical quest bodies) resolves by shipping a pure-convention layer: the writ row holds just the Goal plus a pointer warning comment; the living body lives in a real file at `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<id>.md`; git in the vibers guild provides history and conflict semantics; closure is a manual Coco ritual (snapshot file into row → complete writ → rm file).

That model deliberately has no framework support — no new `nsg` subcommands, no schema changes, no CLI hydration. It is \"us-specific, not framework-proven\" (Sean's framing, 2026-04-10). The tradeoff is that Coco has to remember the ritual every time, and if a future session (or script, or other agent) calls `nsg writ edit --body` on a live quest without reading the visible warning in the row body, data can drift: the file and the row get out of sync, and on closure the wrong version wins.

This wrapper is the escape hatch for that risk. We're deferring it until the pain shows up — the alternative (building it now) violates the us-specific framing and locks in a CLI shape before we've learned what ergonomics actually matter.

Watch items during the parent quest's initial life:

- Does Coco reliably follow the close ritual end-to-end?
- Does the row-body warning comment actually prevent accidental edits?
- Do migration re-runs surface drift, or is drift rare enough to not matter?
- Does any other agent (Astrolabe, ethnographer, future animas) try to touch quest writs in a way that conflicts with the convention?

If the answers are \"yes, yes, no, no,\" the wrapper stays deferred forever. If any of them flip, open this quest and build the thing.

## References

- Parent: File-canonical quest bodies — w-mnt0jin1-960d83b73712
- Source conversation: 2026-04-10 session with Sean resolving M1–M4 on the file-canonical design

## Notes

- 2026-04-10: opened as the deferred M4 escape hatch during the file-canonical quest design conversation. Captured now so the idea doesn't evaporate.

content.text:
Noticed a couple of things while reading the file:

1. **Stale References** — "Parent: File-canonical quest bodies" is now wrong.
2. **Stale Next Steps** — "Wait for the parent quest to ship and land" is stale (w-mnt0jin1 has shipped, just isn't closed yet).
3. **Escaped-quote artifacts** — `\"yes, yes, no, no,\"` and `\"us-specific, not framework-proven\"` leaked through from the original heredoc when I created the quest. Ugly, worth cleaning up while I'm in there.

On the row body: its Goal mentions "parent quest w-mnt0jin1" but in an explanatory clause about design lineage, not structural parentage. The helper CLI *does* exist because of the file-canonical design — that's a design-lineage fact that's still true. The structural parent (in the writs tree) is now the substrate quest, which is a separate axis. I'll leave the row alone, matching your call.

attachment.snippet:
227	### Reopening a closed quest (rare)
228	
229	If you need to reopen a closed quest, extract the row body back into a fresh file at `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<writ-id>.md`, then replace the row body with the generic live-quest stub (warning comments + Goal section only). Reset the status via the appropriate `nsg writ` transition. Commit the resurrected file in the vibers repo.
230	
231	## Quests and session continuity
232	
233	Quests provide continuity across sessions. The flow is:
234	
235	- **Startup** — scan open quest titles with `nsg writ list --type quest --status ready --status active --status waiting --limit 100`; load bodies on demand by reading `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<writ-id>.md` for the quest Sean references. The `--limit 100` is load-bearing: the CLI's default limit is 20, and a silently-truncated list will give you a wrong picture of the board. Raise the limit further if 100 stops being enough.
236	- **During the session** — open new quests for substantial inquiries; update existing ones as thinking evolves. Keep Notes as a running scratchpad.
237	- **Wrap-up** — for any quest touched this session: drain Notes into Context, refresh Status, refresh Next Steps. The next session should be able to resume cold by reading Goal / Status / Next Steps without needing the transcript.
238	
239	Quests are visible to other agents via the Clerk — notably Astrolabe, which may eventually use them as planning inputs. While a quest is live, an agent reading the row sees only the Goal stub and the warning comments pointing at the skill; to read the full living body, the agent must open the file at `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<writ-id>.md`. After closure, the full snapshot is in the row. Write quest bodies assuming an autonomous planning agent may read them.
240	
241	## The row-body-edit trap
242	
243	The most likely way this convention fails is: a future session (Coco or another agent) calls `nsg writ edit --body` on a live quest without reading the warning comments first, silently overwriting the row body. The file still holds the real content, so the visible damage is limited — until closure, when the Coco ritual snapshots the file into the row and the interim row-edit is lost anyway. The trap is mostly benign because the file is the source of truth.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Quest-helper CLI wrapper for nsg

## Goal

Build a small CLI that wraps `nsg` with a quest-shaped interface for Coco (and other LLM agents working on quests). The wrapper encodes the file-canonical quest conventions as commands, so individual rituals (create, open for edit, close, migrate) can't drift or be executed out of order. This is a sanctum-side convenience layer, not a framework feature — it exists because the file-canonical quest model (parent quest w-mnt0jin1) is a convention layered on top of `nsg`, and discipline alone may not be enough to keep the convention consistent over time.

## Status

deferred (2026-04-10) — the file-canonical quest convention (w-mnt0jin1) has shipped without CLI scaffolding. This quest now sits in watch-for-drift mode. Revisit only if the convention proves too easy to get wrong in practice.

## Next Steps

Watch for drift during the file-canonical convention's bedding-in period: Coco forgetting to snapshot on closure, row bodies getting overwritten by accident, migration re-runs surfacing inconsistencies, files appearing in the wrong directory, stale files left behind after closure. If any of those become recurring pain, promote this quest to `active` and scope the wrapper.

When the time comes, the likely command shape is:

- `quest new --title ... --goal ... [--parent <id>]` — creates the writ row with just the Goal + pointer warning, writes the initial file at `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<id>.md` with the full template, prints the file path.
- `quest path <id>` — prints the canonical file path for a quest. Useful for scripts and editor integration.
- `quest close <id> [--complete|--cancel|--fail]` — reads the file, snapshots it into the row body via `nsg writ edit`, transitions status via `nsg writ complete|cancel|fail`, unlinks the file. Atomic-ish from the caller's view.
- `quest migrate` — idempotent one-shot that reconciles row bodies and files for any drift. Safe to re-run.
- `quest list` — thin wrapper around `nsg writ list --type quest` with friendlier output (title + status + one-line Goal preview).

Implementation language: probably a bash script or a small Node script that shells out to `nsg`. No framework changes. Lives in the sanctum (`/workspace/nexus-mk2/bin/quest.sh` or similar).

## Context

Parent quest w-mnt0jin1 (File-canonical quest bodies) resolves by shipping a pure-convention layer: the writ row holds just the Goal plus a pointer warning comment; the living body lives in a real file at `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<id>.md`; git in the vibers guild provides history and conflict semantics; closure is a manual Coco ritual (snapshot file into row → complete writ → rm file).

That model deliberately has no framework support — no new `nsg` subcommands, no schema changes, no CLI hydration. It is "us-specific, not framework-proven" (Sean's framing, 2026-04-10). The tradeoff is that Coco has to remember the ritual every time, and if a future session (or script, or other agent) calls `nsg writ edit --body` on a live quest without reading the visible warning in the row body, data can drift: the file and the row get out of sync, and on closure the wrong version wins.

This wrapper is the escape hatch for that risk. We're deferring it until the pain shows up — the alternative (building it now) violates the us-specific framing and locks in a CLI shape before we've learned what ergonomics actually matter.

Watch items during the parent quest's initial life:

- Does Coco reliably follow the close ritual end-to-end?
- Does the row-body warning comment actually prevent accidental edits?
- Do migration re-runs surface drift, or is drift rare enough to not matter?
- Does any other agent (Astrolabe, ethnographer, future animas) try to touch quest writs in a way that conflicts with the convention?

If the answers are "yes, yes, no, no," the wrapper stays deferred forever. If any of them flip, open this quest and build the thing.

## References

- Parent: File-canonical quest bodies — w-mnt0jin1-960d83b73712
- Source conversation: 2026-04-10 session with Sean resolving M1–M4 on the file-canonical design

## Notes

- 2026-04-10: opened as the deferred M4 escape hatch during the file-canonical quest design conversation. Captured now so the idea doesn't evaporate.

# Wrap-Up: Complete Work and Generate Session Summary

## Generate Summary

Generate a structured summary of the current session. This summary serves one audience:

**The Scribe** — as input for session notes and published material.

Session-to-session continuity is now carried by **quests** (see Coco's agent file). Before generating the wrap-up summary, make sure any quest you touched this session has an up-to-date **Summary** section in its body — that's what the next Coco session will read, not this wrap-up file.

<expected-format>
# Session Summary

## What we did

- Bulleted list of concrete actions taken (files created/modified, decisions made, commits pushed). Reference specific files and commit hashes.

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- What was decided, including explicit "we chose NOT to" decisions. Brief rationale for each.

## Next steps & open questions

- Concrete actions for the next session or for agents to pick up.
- Open questions which were raised but not answered

### Notable moments

Capture quotes, insights, or exchanges that stand out — either for system design or for the research/experiment narrative.

For each notable moment:
- Include **direct quotes from Sean** when available
- Flag **why it's notable** (e.g., "shifted the project's core abstraction", "surfaced a tension between X and Y", "revealed an assumption about how agents should work")
- Prioritize moments where the human changed direction, challenged an assumption, or articulated something new about the project's purpose

### Experiment observations

Flag anything relevant to active experiments. Omit this section if nothing applies.

<expected-format>

## Complete Work

After wrapping up, **ALWAYS**:

- Commit any changes you have made, if any
- Push main to the remote

## Reminders

Check for things Sean should be nudged about:

- **Ethnographer interview:** Check the most recent interview file in `experiments/ethnography/interviews/` (files named `YYYY-MM-DDTHHMMSS.md`). If the most recent one is more than a day old — or if none exist yet — remind Sean to run an ethnographer session. Keep it light: *"It's been a couple days since your last ethnographer interview — might be a good time to check in."*

## Guidelines

- Keep the whole summary under ~500 words. Brevity is a feature.
- Do not editorialize beyond the "why it's notable" flags.
- Write the summary to `.scratch/recent-sessions/<timestamp>.md`, where `<timestamp>` is the current date and time formatted as `YYYY-MM-DDTHHMMSS.md` (use the actual current time — don't round to the nearest hour). Each session gets its own file — never overwrite a previous one.
- Write the summary to `experiments/ethnography/session-notes/new/<timestamp>.md`. where `<timestamp>` is the current date and time formatted as `YYYY-MM-DDTHHMMSS.md` (use the actual current time — don't round to the nearest hour). Each session gets its own file — never overwrite a previous one. This file is for the ethnographer and must NOT be auto-deleted — it accumulates until the ethnographer reviews it.
- Commit all `session-notes`, and `experiments/data/transcripts/` changes to git and push those changes. The transcripts directory may contain files from concurrent sessions — commit whatever is there.
- In addition to writing the summary, share it in the chat for review and inclusion in session transcripts

toolUseResult.content:
# Session Summary

## What we did

- **Moved quest w-mnt0jin1 "File-canonical quest bodies" forward from design to shipped.** Resolved the three open design questions (Q1 flat layout, Q2 Sean's status-lifecycle framing, Q3 id-only filenames) and four micro-decisions (M1 pure convention, M2 `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/`, M3 Goal-only row body, M4 helper CLI deferred). On Sean's push, the design was **reframed from a framework change to a pure sanctum-side convention** — no `nsg` subcommands, no schema changes.
- **Spawned child quest w-mnt106rv** "Quest-helper CLI wrapper for nsg" as the deferred M4 escape hatch. Later re-parented it via direct sqlite patch on `books_clerk_writs.content` JSON — moved from child of w-mnt0jin1 to child of w-mnswvmj7 (Writ substrate), so the helper CLI is a peer of the file-canonical quest under the substrate umbrella rather than a grandchild.
- **Git-initialized `/workspace/vibers/`** (root commit `ecc2509`) with `.gitignore` covering `.nexus/`, `node_modules/`, `tmp/`. First time the guild workspace has been under version control.
- **Wrote `bin/migrate-quest-bodies.sh`** — idempotent, `--dry-run` flag, extracts live quest bodies to `writs/quests/<id>.md` in vibers and replaces row bodies with a generic warning-comment + Goal stub. Ran against all 38 live quests (vibers commit `1fbc60a`).
- **Updated `.claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md`** with the split-body model, path convention, four-step open/close rituals, and the row-body-edit trap section. Sean added a `--limit 100` note to the startup command after noticing the default-20 truncation would silently hide most of the board.
- **Sanctum commit `c7ff945`**: skill update + migration script + coco-log entry.
- **Vibers commits**: `5d40620` (defining quest body updated to shipped state), `b830fba` (helper-CLI quest body updated after re-parent).

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- **File-canonical quest bodies: pure convention, not framework feature.** Sean's reframe: *"I think, for now, this whole system is very 'us-specific' and not 'framework-proven'."* Collapsed a sprawling CLI-hydration design into a convention Coco maintains.
- **Row body stub is path-free.** Sean caught Coco drifting from "pure convention" by putting the path in the warning comment. Corrected to a generic skill-file pointer, which collapsed the create ritual from five steps to four and one `nsg` call.
- **Defining quest w-mnt0jin1 left in `waiting`, not closed.** Coco's lean: let the convention bed in for a session or two before the first real closure-ritual test, so the defining quest isn't its own test case.
- **Helper CLI deferred.** Build it only if drift bites in practice.

## Next steps & open questions

- **Close quest w-mnt0jin1** in a later session via the four-step ritual, once the convention has been exercised a few times.
- **Watch for drift** during the convention's bedding-in period: snapshot forgetting, accidental `nsg writ edit --body` calls on live quests, stale files left behind.
- **Vibers guild git hygiene is now a live concern** — who commits, at what cadence, and how agents other than Coco handle the repo is undefined.

### Notable moments

- **Sean's reframe from framework-change to convention.** *"I wasn't necessarily thinking there would be any CLI support... the body for in-progress writs would contain the static info (goals only maybe?) and coco would find the rest on disk using naming conventions."* Notable because it dissolved most of Coco's proposed design and made the implementation land in one session instead of multiple. Surfaced an implicit rule: until something is framework-proven, keep it in the sanctum as convention.
- **Sean catching the pure-convention drift.** *"i thought we weren't putting file paths in the rits at all?"* Small but sharp — Coco had slipped a path into the warning comment and rationalized it as a placeholder. The correction made the create ritual dramatically simpler. Evidence that Sean holds design constraints more consistently than Coco does.
- **Direct DB patch over CLI.** Sean asked to re-parent the helper-CLI quest with no CLI command available. Coco patched `books_clerk_writs.content` JSON directly via better-sqlite3. Notable as a data point on the friction cost of missing CLI operations — worth watching whether "write a script" becomes the default fix vs. commissioning CLI features.

### Experiment observations

Nothing directly tied to an active experiment, but the session is a clean instance of **interface friction as diagnostic signal for structural mismatch** (noted in w-mnt0jin1's References as worth a published-work note). Editing pain with the old mutable-row body was the symptom; the structural mismatch was that synthesized narrative wanted file affordances, not row affordances.

## What we did

- **Moved quest w-mnt0jin1 "File-canonical quest bodies" from design to shipped.** Resolved Q1–Q3 (flat layout, status-driven lifecycle, id-only filenames) and M1–M4 (pure convention, vibers `writs/quests/`, Goal-only row body, helper CLI deferred). Reframed mid-conversation from a framework change to a pure sanctum-side convention on Sean's push.
- **Spawned child quest w-mnt106rv "Quest-helper CLI wrapper for nsg"** as the deferred M4 escape hatch. Later re-parented via direct sqlite patch on `books_clerk_writs.content` JSON — moved from child of w-mnt0jin1 to child of w-mnswvmj7 (Writ substrate).
